info.text: In this demo we'll use the netnet-standard-library (nn.min.js) to create a button that plays audio from a file. Of course, you can do all of this with the browser's native APIs, JavaScript libraries are just abstractions that reduce boilerplate and let us express our ideas with less code.
info.title: getting started
script tags
info.text: In order to use the nn object, we'll first need to import the library using a script tag, then in a separate script element we'll write our code, starting first with the /* global nn */ comment so netnet knows we've imported a new global variable in our imported library.

info.title: audio element
info.text: Here I've used the nn.create() method to create a new "audio" element and chained the .set() method to set that element's "src" and "volume" attributes. I'm storing the element in a new variable I've crated called sound so I can reference it below. The sound file itself was created by orangefreesounds

info.title: button element
info.text: Here I've used the nn.create() method again to create a "button" element and chained a few functions.

info.title: content
info.text: The .content() method changes the text content of the button and then .addTo() injects this button element into the body.

info.title: position
info.text: The .positionOrigin() method is used to set our origin point to the center of the button (rather than the top/left which is the default) so that when we set the .position() to the center of the screen (half the screen's width and height) the button also appears centered.

info.title: click event
info.text: Lastly, we attach an event listener to the button's "click" event, which calls the audio element's .play() method when we click the button.

info.title: other approaches
info.text: We could also do this with the Web Audio API if we want lower level control over the audio data as well as the Tone.js library if we want to make use of all it's methods.
